glossary.def: A one-parameter forecaster that maintains a level as a weighted average of past observations whose weights decay geometrically into the past; the level itself is the (flat) forecast for every horizon.
The learning rate of an exponential smoother, between 0 and 1. Large α gives short memory and tracks recent data closely; small α gives long memory and behaves like a slow long-run average.
Double exponential smoothing: alongside the level it smooths a trend component (change per period) with its own parameter β, so the forecast is a straight line rather than a flat one.

A modification of Holt's method that multiplies the projected trend by a factor φ<1 each step, so long-horizon forecasts bend toward a finite asymptote instead of running off linearly — a perennial forecasting-competition benchmark.
Triple exponential smoothing: level, trend, and a vector of m seasonal indices updated by γ. Comes in additive (constant-size season) and multiplicative (season grows with the level) variants.

The innovations state-space framework (Error, Trend, Season) that gives every exponential-smoothing method an underlying probability model with a single source of error — yielding a likelihood, AIC-based model selection, and exact prediction intervals.

A per-period adjustment (one per slot in the seasonal cycle) that captures the recurring calendar pattern; additive indices are normalized to sum to zero per cycle, multiplicative ones to average one.

The standard way to fit smoothing parameters and initial states: numerically minimize the in-sample sum of squared one-step-ahead forecast errors, equivalent to maximizing the Gaussian likelihood of the ETS model.

refs.cite: Holt, C. C. (2004, orig. 1957). Forecasting seasonals and trends by exponentially weighted moving averages. International Journal of Forecasting 20(1).
Winters, P. R. (1960). Forecasting sales by exponentially weighted moving averages. Management Science 6(3).
Hyndman, R. J., Koehler, A. B., Ord, J. K. & Snyder, R. D. (2008). Forecasting with Exponential Smoothing: The State Space Approach. Springer.
Hyndman, R. J., Koehler, A. B., Snyder, R. D. & Grose, S. (2002). A state space framework for automatic forecasting using exponential smoothing methods. International Journal of Forecasting 18(3).
Gardner, E. S. & McKenzie, E. (1985). Forecasting trends in time series. Management Science 31(10).
Makridakis, S., Spiliotis, E. & Assimakopoulos, V. (2020). The M4 Competition: 100,000 time series and 61 forecasting methods. International Journal of Forecasting 36(1).
Hyndman, R. J. & Athanasopoulos, G. (2021). Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed.), Ch. 8. OTexts.
